file_name: Sarker et al. - 2026 - LGGC-Net a local-global graph and color attention-based lightweight CNN for skin cancer classificat.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper proposes LGGC-Net, a lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) with local-global graph and color attention mechanisms, to perform skin cancer classification (binary and multiclass) on dermoscopic images from the ISIC Archive (SKIN2025, HAM10000, ISIC 2019 datasets).

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes a novel lightweight CNN architecture named LGGC-Net and presents original experimental results, including performance metrics (accuracy, AUC), ablation studies, and evaluations on specific datasets (SKIN2025, HAM10000, ISIC 2019, DST-50). It includes a methods section describing the model design and a results section detailing new findings, distinguishing it from a review paper.

title: LGGC-Net: A Local-Global Graph and Color Attention-Based Lightweight CNN for Skin Cancer Classification
brief_summary: This study proposes LGGC-Net, a lightweight Convolutional Neural Network integrating local-global graph and color attention mechanisms for skin cancer classification. The model was evaluated on binary and multiclass tasks using datasets from the ISIC Archive, including SKIN2025, HAM10000, and a diverse skin tone dataset (DST-50). In binary classification, LGGC-Net achieved 88.05% accuracy with only 0.81 million parameters, while attaining 76.1% accuracy in multiclass settings on the HAM10000 dataset. The model consistently outperformed existing methods on deployment-oriented metrics, such as accuracy per epoch and per million parameters, with an area under the curve exceeding 0.93 in both cases. Explainability was assessed using Grad-CAM++ and SHAP, confirming the model's interpretability. The results indicate that LGGC-Net offers an effective, efficient, and potentially deployment-ready solution for practical skin cancer screening.

lesion_types_studied.details: Benign class in binary classification; one of seven subtypes in multiclass classification (12,875 images in ISIC 2019, 6,705 in HAM10000).
Benign class in binary classification; one of seven subtypes in multiclass classification (2,624 images in ISIC 2019, 1,099 in HAM10000).
Benign class in binary classification; one of seven subtypes in multiclass classification (167 images in ISIC 2019, 115 in HAM10000).
Benign class in binary classification; one of seven subtypes in multiclass classification (177 images in ISIC 2019, 142 in HAM10000).
Malignant class in binary classification; one of seven subtypes in multiclass classification (4,522 images in ISIC 2019, 1,113 in HAM10000).
Malignant class in binary classification; one of seven subtypes in multiclass classification (3,323 images in ISIC 2019, 514 in HAM10000).
Premalignant class grouped with malignant in binary classification; one of seven subtypes in multiclass classification (607 images in ISIC 2019, 327 in HAM10000).

Small number of SCC images present in the malignant set of SKIN2025; grouped with AK category. Explicitly noted as absent from the AK class of HAM10000 and not labeled as a separate class in ISIC datasets.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification (benign vs. malignant) and multiclass classification (seven skin lesion subtypes: NV, BKL, DF, VASC, MEL, BCC, AK)
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification (distinguishing benign from malignant lesions) and multiclass classification (identifying seven specific lesion subtypes) using dermoscopic images. The model predicts discrete diagnostic categories. Metrics reported include accuracy, AUC, precision, recall, and F1-score.

datasets_used.name: SKIN2025
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset created by the authors, randomly selected from the ISIC Archive, comprising 10,000 benign and 10,000 malignant dermoscopic images. It was used for binary classification experiments.
classes_used: Malignant (MEL, BCC, AK, SCC grouped with AK)

use.training: Used for training binary classification models (60% split).
use.validation: Used for validation of binary classification models (20% split).
use.testing: Used for testing binary classification models (20% split).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A public dataset from the ISIC Archive comprising 24,703 dermoscopic images spanning seven lesion subtypes. Used for multiclass classification experiments.

use.training: Used for training multiclass classification models (70% split).
use.validation: Used for validation of multiclass classification models (15% split).
use.testing: Used for testing multiclass classification models (15% split).

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public benchmark dataset from the ISIC Archive used for external evaluation to assess robustness and generalization. It contains images from the same patient population as ISIC 2019 but was not used during training or validation.

use.testing: Used for external testing of multiclass classification models (unseen data).
use.other: Used for external testing of binary classification models (8,000 benign, 2,000 malignant selected).

datasets_used.name: DST-50
datasets_used.description: A custom dataset prepared by the authors comprising 100 dermoscopic images (50 benign, 50 malignant) with diverse skin tones (Fitzpatrick skin types III–VI) collected from different patient populations. Used to validate robustness under domain shift conditions.

use.testing: Used for external testing of binary classification models to assess generalization across diverse skin tones.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To ensure reproducibility and fair comparison of models with and without LGGC attention modules.
To assess the robustness and variance of model performance across different training initializations.
To report the central tendency and variability of accuracy metrics across multiple runs with different random seeds.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, t-tests, ANOVA) for statistical significance. Performance comparisons were made using mean accuracy and standard deviation across multiple random seeds, and stability metrics like the Stability Index (SI) and F1 Difference Ratio, but no significance tests were employed.

SKIN2025.notes: LGGC-Net achieved the highest overall performance. Integrating LGGC attention consistently improved precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy across all baseline CNNs. The model maintained stable accuracy and outperformed existing methods on deployment-oriented metrics.

LGGC-Net.notes: Demonstrated superior robustness and generalization capability under heterogeneous data conditions with significant improvements in recall and F1-score.

HAM10000.notes: HAM10000 was used as an external test set for unseen data. LGGC-Net showed clear robustness improvements, particularly in reducing false negatives. DenseNet121 with LGGC attention achieved the strongest and most balanced performance on HAM10000.

DST-50.notes: DST-50 comprised 50 benign and 50 malignant images with diverse skin tones (Fitzpatrick skin types III–VI). LGGC-Net performed unbiasedly across diverse skin tones. Grad-CAM++ visualizations confirmed generalization ability across the entire Fitzpatrick skin tone scale (I–VI).

ISIC_2019.notes: Models were trained and validated on ISIC 2019. LGGC attention consistently improved performance across all architectures. DenseNet121 and MobileNetV2 showed substantial gains in F1-score.

preprocessing_techniques: Random selection of images from the ISIC Archive to create the SKIN2025 dataset (10,000 benign and 10,000 malignant images)
Grouping of Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) images with the Actinic Keratosis (AK) category in the malignant class
Selection of specific subsets from HAM10000 for external evaluation (8,000 benign and 2,000 malignant for binary; 10,015 images for multiclass)
Creation of the DST-50 dataset comprising 50 benign and 50 malignant images with diverse skin tones (Fitzpatrick skin types III–VI)
Extraction of skin regions to compute Individual Typology Angle (ITA) scores
Conversion of images to the CIE Lab color space for skin tone analysis

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied with a range of ±10 degrees in offline learning; 10 degrees in online learning.
Applied with a shift factor of 0.1.
Applied with a zoom factor of 0.1.
Used for handling padding during augmentation in the online learning environment.

conclusions: LGGC-Net, a lightweight CNN integrating local-global graph and color attention mechanisms, is an effective and interpretable solution for skin cancer classification.

The LGGC attention module consistently improves classification performance (precision, recall, F1-score, and accuracy) across various CNN backbones (DenseNet121, EfficientNetB0, ResNet50, MobileNetV2) for both binary and multiclass tasks.

In binary classification on the SKIN2025 dataset, LGGC-Net achieved 88.05% accuracy with only 0.81 million parameters and an AUC exceeding 0.93, demonstrating high efficiency and discriminative power.

In multiclass classification on the unseen HAM10000 dataset, LGGC-Net attained 76.1% accuracy, showing superior robustness and generalization capability under heterogeneous data conditions compared to existing methods.
LGGC-Net demonstrated strong generalization across diverse skin tones (Fitzpatrick skin types III–VI) on the custom DST-50 dataset, achieving 85% accuracy and unbiased performance.

Ablation studies confirmed that the complete LGGC attention module yields the most reliable predictions, with color and local attentions being critical for distinguishing lesion-specific features, while channel and graph attentions provide complementary global and spatial information.

The model maintains stable accuracy and outperforms existing methods on deployment-oriented metrics (e.g., accuracy per epoch, accuracy per million parameters), making it suitable for resource-constrained clinical environments.
Explainability assessments using Grad-CAM++ and SHAP confirmed the model's interpretability and clinical relevance.
important_remarks: The SKIN2025 dataset is a custom dataset created by the authors (randomly selected from ISIC Archive), not a standard public benchmark, which limits direct comparability with prior literature.
The DST-50 external validation dataset is extremely small (only 100 images total), providing limited statistical power for assessing generalization across diverse skin tones.

The HAM10000 dataset used for external evaluation shares the same patient population and predominantly light skin tones (Fitzpatrick types I-II) as the training data (ISIC 2019), limiting the assessment of true domain shift robustness.

Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) images are grouped with Actinic Keratosis (AK) in the malignant class for ISIC datasets, and invasive SCC is not explicitly labeled as a separate class, potentially obscuring specific diagnostic performance for SCC.
The paper does not report formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) for significance; comparisons rely on mean accuracy and standard deviation across random seeds.
The manuscript is an unedited 'Article in Press' version, which may contain errors or content changes before final publication.
Ethical approval was waived as the study used fully de-identified, publicly available data, meaning no direct clinical validation or patient interaction was performed.
